sentences.text: Yes, I was interrogated when I was arrested by the Germans, in October, 1943.
Well, I was supposed to meet a girl at the railroad station in Heerlen.
That's H-E-E-R-L-E-N.
And when I walked around, in front of the railway station, and all of a guy came to me.
And he said, are you Arie, he said.
I said, yes.
He said, don't go away, because the girl is arrested by the Germans.
And when I turn around to walk away from that, there was two strong men holding  my arms, put me in the handcuffs.
And that was the end.
Yeah, it was one of the-- one of those guys that was either [?
Fassenhout ?] or [?
Wiesmauer, ?] one of the two, with traitors, yes.
One of the collaborators, yes.
I was put into a car.
And we waited for a while to pick up another guy.
And that was not the case.
So they had another guy with me who also meeting that girl, because he wanted to go-- to go to France to work in France underground.
And they arrested him, too.
So we both were transferred to the headquarters of the local Gestapo in Maastricht.
They were Gestapo agents, yeah, Germans.
Germans, yes.
No.
They were not in uniform, no.
The took me to the headquarters there.
And first, they locked me up in the basement.
They had  a kind of cellar, there, a kind of cell.
And then, later on, they interrogated me.
And they wanted to find out the name, where the hiding place was from a man by the name of Zroemen, Z-R-O-E-M-E-N.
And another guy who they were searching for is Bongaarts, B-O-N-G-A-A-R-T-S.
And I didn't know that.
But they kept on hitting me and kept hitting me-- and-- and my throat was kind of swollen here-- until like I fell unconscious.
I-- And I went on the floor.
Then they let me go and brought me back to the cellar downstairs.
Just for a-- for a half an hour.
And I was lucky, because I just had some pictures from Jewish people in my pocket for new ID cards.
And in my handcuffed hands, I could go into my pocket and stuff the pictures in the chair, under the chair.
And so they couldn't find them.
It was just in the cellar.
It was--
The cellar, yeah.
And it was just a small room they have to [INAUDIBLE] or lock up people.
No, no, I was  the only one there, yeah.
Well, the only thought I had was that I hoped that they never could find the people who I'm hiding.
And that was my only concern here.
I expected the worst, was all I know.
But I took the consequences.
I had no problem with that.
But I didn't want to-- any other-- anybody else to be involved.
That's correct, for parents and-- and they, of course, nobody knew that I was arrested.
But it happened to be that, and travel agent who happens to be at the railway station.
And he saw that.
And he just went to [?
John ?] [?
Boss, ?] as I've mentioned before.
And so-- and then they came to know that I was arrested there.
They brought  me upstairs, and then they interrogated me, and hit  me.
There was one guy, this particular guy, from the other day, by the name of Nitch, N-I-T-C-H.
And he hit me with his hand, [? on ?] my throat, all the time, the same spot.
He was the one who was [INAUDIBLE] the hitting part.
And there was another one that tried to get out of me where [?
Zroemen ?] was and where Bongaart was.
And I didn't know that.
He hit me to make me speak, yeah.
It swelled up, yes.
Yeah.
Then when I came back to normal, they brought me back to the cellar, downstairs.
And in the afternoon, they put me in a car, with more Gestapo people, and they drove me to the city of Scheveningen.
That's close to The Hague.
And Scheveningen is spelled S-C-H-E-V-E-N-I-N-G-E-N.
And there, they put me in a cell.
No, nothing.
I was privileged, of course, that they found, also, quite a few [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] food stamps, sheets of food stamps in my briefcase.
I had them.
I used to send them to people-- they could not trace it-- in Amsterdam, a couple families, and a couple of families in Utrecht.
And they were addressed to mail to them.
They found them.
And they asked me what it meant.
And I told them, well, I knew, it was a search on the railroad station, they would not open up envelopes.
So we had wanted to put it in an envelope and put in an address, his friends, [INAUDIBLE] just any address on there.
They would accept that.
And they accepted my story.
So.
Yeah.
Yeah, and then later on they brought me to Scheveningen, and then yeah.
Yeah.
That was [INAUDIBLE] in The Hague.
It was about three, three and half hours by car.
And there I was put in cell until the last couple of days.
And then they brought me to in prison, Haaren.
That's H-A-A-R-E-N.
And it used to be a monastery.
And they-- they converted it to a prison.
And there, I was locked up, yeah.
In Haaren, yes.
And there, I was in solitary confinement for six months.
Yeah, that's right.
Six months, yeah.
Well, if you know what [INAUDIBLE] you're not, you're not-- you know, you're not afraid.
I mean, I walked around there.
I did my exercise.
I walked around and accepted the food they brought in there.
And then, after, the interrogation started by an-- an officer of the Gestapo.
Well, I had to sit on  the table, you know, and I would tell them a story.
And I  would make up a story.
And they put it down.
And they don't have to sign it.
It was my story.
So.
Fake, yeah.
Fake story, yeah.
They believed me.
And they could-- because I was lucky.
I found another gentleman from Maastricht, who was arrested before me, by the name of Rademakers.
And that's R-A-D-E-M-A-K-E-R-S.
And he was arrested before.
And I've tried to kept contact with him.
I didn't know he was my neighbor in-- in the prison.
But I found out it was he who was my neighbor.
And then we tried to contact each other through the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] tap, to the water tap, you know?
If you open up the tap, that's you hear a [? sissing ?] sound.
And that way I-- I yelled through the wall to him.
That A was 1 and B was 2 and so on and so on.
So we did.
We chatted with each other.
So you had kind of your own Morse code?
That's right.
I was one of [INAUDIBLE] first persons to [INAUDIBLE] in there, but anyhow.
And that way, if he was interrogated, he told me what he told them.
And if it was I who was interrogated, I told him what, and so that we could tell the same story to the-- to the guy.
Yeah.
Yeah.
And sometimes, you know, if it was 12 o'clock, before 12 o'clock, we'd-- we set up a menu of   what we would get.
Something like that, yeah.
Not after that, anymore.
The first torture was placed-- took place when I was arrested.
After that, nothing more.
Yeah.
That's right, yes.
About-- about a year.
Yeah.
A year.
How old were you at that time?
When I was arrested, I was 23.
23 years old.
How did you spend the time?
You communicated with the man next door.
I communicated with the man next door.
I walked around.
I read my Bible.
I was allowed to keep that.
I-- sometimes took parcels from home, through the Red Cross, of course.
And I walked around.
I did a lot of walking.
Got some exercises there, in the cell, itself.
I know that I was lucky, because the books, from the-- the-- all the Catholic priests, who used to be in that seminary there, were still in the library there.
And we allowed to take a book every week, to read a book.
So I could do quite a bit of reading there.
Yes, especially the Old Testament, how the Lord helped the Jewish people, all the time out of if they were in need and, they had problems.
And that comforted me, because I expected that it would happen to me, too.
For example, yeah, Job.
But most of the time, mostly what spoke to me were the Psalms, yeah.
The Psalms, yeah.
Yes.
OK.
Did you ever lose faith?
No.
No, no.
That's right, yeah.
No, no.
The only thing-- the only [INAUDIBLE] punishment was, of course, solitary confinement.
That was it, you know.
And you were allowed to get some fresh air for five minutes.
And they let you out, cell by cell.
And then I was brought back again.
So.
And then at night-- the night, of course, the Germans soldier opened up the door, an SS soldier.
And there were two soldiers, as a matter of fact.
And you had to put all your-- all your clothes outside the cell.
And in the morning, you weren't allowed to take it in again.
Of course, you're afraid if [? they'd kill ?] [? you. ?] And you'd find a way to get out of the prison there.
No, except there would be underwear, that's all.
Yeah, it was.
Yeah, it was heated.
But it was, of course, looked dirty, because you had no opportunity to wash the [INAUDIBLE] my cell, sometimes, with the little tap that there was.
Half of this room, here, yeah, if you stop it here.
Yeah, something like that, yes.
There was a bed in there and a wooden chair.
And there was a water tap there.
And that was about it.
And we got a wooden spoon.
And we got a wooden knife for your food.
And there was a bowl that you put your food in, but  I had also to use it for drinking coffee.
You got to just put it.
Yes.
A wooden knife and spoon--
That's right, yeah.
Yeah.
Not too bad.
It was done by Dutch cooks there.
And usually it was mixed up vegetable and potatoes.
Hardly any meat, of course.
But because we were in the middle of an orchard, we got quite a few, through the Red Cross, apples to eat.
And it was a kind of blessing.
Yeah.
Yeah.
There, in the prison, yes, yeah.
No, no, you had to stay in your cell.
Yeah.
You opened the door.
And you had to hand-- [INAUDIBLE] your hand out, and do it with the bowl.
And they put your food in there.
And that was it.
Germans, German soldiers.
The Green Polizei.
Yeah.
They were all right.
[INAUDIBLE] These people were all right.
Yeah.
They were just people, ordinary people.
And you could talk to them, yeah.
Well, quite a few, yeah, quite a few.
A couple of hundred, I'd suppose, yeah.
Yeah, something like that.
What kind of prisoners?
You know, they were-- many of them were involved in espionage.
Not as far as I know, no.
No, no, not at all, no, no, no.
Well, they brought me from the prison, after six months, to a concentration camp.
In Amersfoort.
Yeah, A-M-E-R-S-F-O-O-R-T.
The center of Holland.
Yeah.
And there was a concentration camp.
And he brought me there.
And there was maybe 4,000 or 5,000 prisoners in that camp.
The majority were people who were going to go back to Germany, of course, if they worked there.
But if you was a political prisoner, like I was, they sew on  the back of your jacket a red ball.
They could see you as a political prisoner.
And I had U sewn on my coat, meaning that I was still in-- they were still investigating me.
And they had [INAUDIBLE] number, of course, on your jacket.
Untersuch, yeah.
Untersuch, yeah.
Under investigation, yeah.
Political prisoners.
You are not allowed to go out of the camp.
Like the people who were there to work in Germany, they were allowed to go to work outside of the concentration camp.
We were not allowed.
About 4,000 or 5,000 prisoners were there, you know, in barracks.
No, there was only that kind.
Some-- some black-- who were involved in the black market, we ran into sometimes, yeah.
I saw a few Jews, yes.
As a matter of fact, when the [?
ATF ?] suspected you, that you was a Jew, you have to undress yourself, and they will see if you are circumcised, yes or no.
And they found a couple of guys.
And they had a very, very hard time, yeah.
Very hard time, yeah.
I saw one guy, and he had to go and stand in front for the camp.
And loaded a heavy gun on his arms, and had to make bending knees all the time.
But he fell down.
He couldn't do it anymore.
Well, they brought him to his cell, and then they brought him to another camp.
Yeah, yeah, yeah.
If they-- if they-- they were suspicions, you know, then they-- then they hit-- they hit you quite a bit if there was a Jew, yeah.
I think that the food was terrible.
It was more water than food.
And lots of people had dysentery there.
We got only a little piece of bread.
And you had to live on it that for [INAUDIBLE] the whole day.
So it was [? everybody ?] was [? hungry. ?] A bad time there, yeah.
We lived in barracks, in bunk-beds.
You know, three-- three on top of each other, to [? them-- ?]
Triple bunk-bed.
We had two toilets for 500 people to use.
And in thinking about that, most of them had dysentery.
It was a  mess.
It was terrible.
Yeah.
Yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah. yeah.
No.
We had-- in the morning, we had to stand on the-- on the park-- plaza.
And there was roll-call there.
And [INAUDIBLE] anyone was missing, then you had to stay in the [? sun ?] for hours.
Once a month, you could go to and take a  shower.
There wasn't room with all the showers.
And you took, all together, one shower.
So we had to-- to move to get some water on your body.
And you had to undress in your cell, because there was lots of fleas there, and then the lice, and things like that.
And you got clean clothes after that.
And they were even worse than the ones we had before.
So it was an awful mess there.
No, not all.
Not all, no, no, no, no.
I was  in the concentration camp for about three or four months.
And then they sent me to a prison.
To another prison in Utrecht.
And there I was to appear before a [GERMAN].
A court-- an army court-- what do you call it?
Court martial, yeah.
And they [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] told us that they couldn't find any evidence against me and my friends.
So we were dismissed.
But for our own safety, they didn't let us go, because they were afraid that you're going to start again.
So to protect me, you know, they kept me there.
That was the first   prison, yeah.
And that was around September, 1944.
And then, of course, there came the Allies.
They liberated the southern part of the Netherlands.
And in order to give a good impression, they emptied the  prison for those people.
So like me and others, we were allowed to go home.
I was about three months in that prison, yet, until October, 1944.
OK.
Before you were arrested the first time, were you ever trained in how to be interrogated?
Did they ever give you any training on that?
No, nobody.
And the only thing I had in that cell was a man who was from the Dutch intelligence service.
And when I had to appear before the court, the court martial, he had little pills, pep pills.
And he said, [INAUDIBLE] he take one, he said.
And then you'll be more alert, and you know what you're going to say.
And I took one.
But he said, also, don't answer them in German.
Act if you don't understand German.
So that way, you get a translator, and they give you time to think.
But as a result of that pep pill, I was answering before the guy was finished with translating everything.
It was OK, yeah.
Oh, yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah.
He had not enough evidence.
The [INAUDIBLE] They thought we were trying to liberate a friend, out of the-- Derk Van Assen-- out of the prison.
And we had no success.
They shot Van Assen, but they didn't want to admit   it there.
So they acted, you know, he was still rescued, but not by them.
That's right, yes.
The liberation occurred, for me, not in the southern part of Holland, what happened in September, 1944.
But it happened to me in May 1945, when I was in Utrecht with-- with some family, [INAUDIBLE] for the second time  I was released from prison.
And I stayed with some family.
And there was-- they couldn't stay there anymore, because they had hardly any food.
And I even met my brother there, who were the two, and he brought me to a farmer about 10 kilometers from Utrecht.
And they went-- there we stayed.
And we had plenty of food.
And there, I had the liberation.
Canadian soldiers came on the-- on the road to Utrecht.
The came inside this village there.
And there was an quite a party there, of course.
Everybody scream with pleasure.
No.
Not at all, no.
Not at all.
They brought me-- later on, they brought me home to Maastricht.
And when they entered Maastricht, I go into my own place.
[INAUDIBLE] They spread it around to all the Jewish people who we're helping live there.
And they all came and all run to my house, you know, to show enormous welcome, yeah.
I was excited.
It was, to me, that was the crown on my work, let's put it that way, that I helped these people, and that they could walk around as free men and women in the city of Maastricht, again, yeah.
And I had a part in that, yes.
Yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah.
That's right, yes.
Some member of my family spill it right away.
My sister, I guess, must have done that, right away, to put it back, because we didn't have a telephone at the time, and run out, he's home, he's home.
And they went to our place, you know.
And he embraced me.
And my whole face was all with lipstick and whatever.
[CHUCKLING]
A good memory, yeah, yeah.
Well, all the family who lived there, so there was, maybe, 30, 40 dropped in, you know.
Oh, yes, oh, yeah, yeah.
And at night, there was the [?
G ?] [? corps ?] from band from the community.
They came to our place and gave me serenades.
Yeah.
No.
No.
I am afraid that people think, [?
Bob ?] [?
Wallen ?] and guy is that, a name like that.
So I never spoke about it.
Even my kids didn't know about it till I had a Jewish friend, here, Carl Goldstein.
He's also from Maastricht.
And I always had close contact with him.
And he mentioned my name to the community [INAUDIBLE] in Ottawa, to-- to Mendel Good-- well-known.
And they said, they promised me, say, listen, you should open your mouth now, because antisemitism is coming up again.
And people should know, witnesses, what happened, during the war time.
They convinced me that it was time to speak up, yes.
No, no, no, no.
I don't like that they have the story.
And I always had the feeling that they think that he's overdoing it, you know?
So that's why I keep quiet about it, yeah.
Oh, they found  it a fantastic idea.
They were excited about it.
Well, they listened to my stories, of course.
And I got sick and tired of telling the same story all the time.
So then I thought, well, if I can put it in a booklet, maybe they can actually read it.
And I found a ad in a-- in a magazine for seniors that someone was printing life stories, for seniors for the family.
And I give them a call and asked for informations.
And he came and told me what it would cost me if he did that.
And then a week later, he called me.
My wife wants to see  you.
She is a professional writer, and she wants to write a book about you.
And that way she started to write my book.
But that was later on.
Janet Keith.
Janet Keith, yeah.
I visited her once or twice a week.
And she didn't know the computer word processor.
And we did it on an old IBM.
But she just typed the whole story, what I told her.
And then, a week later, she wrote it down a week later.
We re-read it, and we corrected it.
And so-- And what she did, of course, I give-- I supplied her with all the addresses for the Jewish people in Maastricht and also in Israel, and in Belgium.
And she wrote them letters asking for their report.
And that, she make, combined into one book.
Oh, a couple of years, yes.
A couple of years.
What is the title of the book?
That's right, yes.
That was my story, yes, and the story of the people I helped, also, yeah.
Well, I don't think that it-- I think they sold out now.
But it is in the libraries, here, and also in the Jewish library in-- in community halls around here.
Yes.
And when was it published?
In 1992, I believe, yeah.
Do you know what happened to the Jews that you sheltered?
What happened to them later on.
Do you  know?
Oh, yes, I do.
I keep in contact with them.
And usually with the Jewish new year, I send them a card or  a letter.
And I get, with our Christmas, a letter back.
They-- and then we've gone three times I went to Israel for a visit.
The first time, I went with a tourist group and let them know that I was coming to Israel.
So we had a reunion in the Hilton Hotel in Jerusalem.
And they were mad at me.
They said, now you go with a tourist group?
[INAUDIBLE] You should have come to us, and we would have driven you around  here and seen something of Israel.
So I had to promise them that I would come back.
And then even after I came back to Israel-- and they even were willing to pay for my trip.
You know, I can afford it.
You don't need to do that.
So the second time I went to Israel, I stayed at their place.
And they brought  me from  one place to the other.
And I had a fantastic time there.
And later on-- that was with my wife, together.
And later on, I went by myself, for two times to Israel, I visit them.
And [INAUDIBLE] I had my birthday a couple of weeks ago, and I got several calls from Israel.
So just part of a family, more or less, you know?
Yes.
And how many people are there in this extended family?
Well, I don't know.
There, in Israel?
Well, roughly, there was about 100.
But the contact I have with them is, of course, a lesser group, because some older people passed away, some moved away, I don't know where.
But the majority I have contact maybe 30, 40, or something like that When I send cards to Israel, there's around 20 that I send every year to them.
That's right, yes.
That's right.
Write letters and whatever, yeah.
OK.
